heroSafety.title: Is LA Safe at Night for Solo Travelers?
heroSafety.subtitle: LA is safe at night in the major tourist areas — and riskier in specific corridors that tourists have no reason to visit. Here's the honest breakdown.

safetyMeter.overallRating: Safe in Tourist Areas with Smart Precautions
safetyMeter.quickSummary: LA's main tourist areas — Santa Monica, West Hollywood, Beverly Hills, and the Hollywood corridor — stay comfortable after dark with normal awareness. Risks increase in specific corridors and when walking residential areas late at night.
avoidIf: Prefer walking everywhere instead of using rideshare
Feel uneasy in spread-out, car-centric cities at night
Plan to explore unfamiliar residential areas after dark on foot

neighborhoodSafetyGrid.name: Santa Monica
neighborhoodSafetyGrid.why: Third Street Promenade and the pier area have foot traffic, open businesses, and security presence at night.

neighborhoodSafetyGrid.name: West Hollywood
neighborhoodSafetyGrid.why: Sunset Strip has consistent foot traffic, good lighting, and visible venue security throughout the night.
neighborhoodSafetyGrid.notes: One of LA's safest nightlife areas.

neighborhoodSafetyGrid.name: Beverly Hills
neighborhoodSafetyGrid.why: Upscale, well-patrolled, and low crime after dark.

neighborhoodSafetyGrid.name: Hollywood Blvd (Tourist Corridor)
neighborhoodSafetyGrid.why: The well-lit tourist corridor stays busy with foot traffic and security.

neighborhoodSafetyGrid.name: DTLA Arts District
neighborhoodSafetyGrid.why: Open businesses and steady foot traffic keep it comfortable after dark.

neighborhoodSafetyGrid.name: Venice Beach Boardwalk
neighborhoodSafetyGrid.why: Lighting is inconsistent and the homeless population increases after dark.
neighborhoodSafetyGrid.notes: Not recommended for solo walking after 10pm.

neighborhoodSafetyGrid.name: East Hollywood
neighborhoodSafetyGrid.why: Quieter and less predictable east of Highland Ave and north of Sunset.

neighborhoodSafetyGrid.name: Malibu Beaches
neighborhoodSafetyGrid.why: Isolated at night with little lighting or foot traffic.
neighborhoodSafetyGrid.notes: Only visit with others and let someone know where you are.

neighborhoodSafetyGrid.name: Skid Row (DTLA)
neighborhoodSafetyGrid.why: High-risk area east of Main St between 3rd and 7th. Tourists have no reason to visit.

neighborhoodSafetyGrid.name: South LA
neighborhoodSafetyGrid.why: Many parts south of the 10 freeway have higher crime and low foot traffic at night.

neighborhoodSafetyGrid.name: Compton
neighborhoodSafetyGrid.why: Higher crime rates at night unless you know the area.

neighborhoodSafetyGrid.name: Watts / Inglewood
neighborhoodSafetyGrid.why: Residential areas best avoided at night unless you know them.

scenarioCards.title: Walking Alone After 10 PM
scenarioCards.whatToExpect: Tourist corridors stay active; residential side streets get very quiet in car-centric LA.
howToStaySafe: Rideshare to your hotel whenever in doubt
scenarioCards.avoidIf: You plan to wander unfamiliar areas on foot

scenarioCards.title: Using Rideshare After 10 PM
scenarioCards.whatToExpect: Fast, reliable, and the standard nighttime choice in LA.
howToStaySafe: Verify license plate before getting in
Use the in-app emergency button if needed
scenarioCards.avoidIf: You're on a very tight budget

scenarioCards.title: Parking at Night
scenarioCards.whatToExpect: Multi-story structures in DTLA and Hollywood can feel isolated late at night.
howToStaySafe: Use the elevator rather than stairwells
Park near the elevator on well-lit floors
Never leave anything visible in your car
Ask venue staff to walk with you if anything feels wrong

scenarioCards.title: Exploring Nightlife Areas
scenarioCards.whatToExpect: Lively venues on the Sunset Strip and DTLA, with quieter blocks just off the main strips.
howToStaySafe: Stay on the main strips with foot traffic
Avoid isolated side streets after leaving venues
Keep drinks in sight at all times
Plan your rideshare home before you head out

nightTips: Rideshare instead of walking unfamiliar areas
Park near elevators on well-lit floors
Keep your phone off your lap at red lights

corePrinciples.title: Default to Rideshare
corePrinciples.description: The single most effective safety measure in LA at night is using Uber or Lyft instead of walking to unfamiliar areas.
corePrinciples.example: Rideshare from venue to hotel whenever in doubt — most locals drive or rideshare at night.

corePrinciples.title: Read the Block
corePrinciples.description: Nighttime safety varies block by block in LA. Scan the lighting, foot traffic, and open businesses around you.
corePrinciples.example: If a block suddenly gets quiet or dark, head back toward the main tourist corridor.

corePrinciples.title: Protect Your Car
corePrinciples.description: Car break-ins are the most common nighttime issue. Never leave anything visible inside your vehicle.
corePrinciples.example: Take your bag, charger, and jacket with you — even a jacket on the seat invites a break-in.

corePrinciples.title: Signal Confidence
corePrinciples.description: Walk with purpose, keep your head up, and avoid behaviors that signal distraction.
corePrinciples.example: Hold your phone at chest level for navigation instead of staring down at it.

corePrinciples.title: Trust Micro-Signals
corePrinciples.description: If something feels off — a person, a block, a parking structure — change course immediately.
corePrinciples.example: If a parking structure feels isolated, go back to the venue and ask staff to walk you out.

rideshare.avoidHours: None — but stay alert after 2 AM
tips: Use the in-app emergency button if needed
recommendedRoutes: Venue to hotel late at night
Any trip into or out of unfamiliar areas

tips: Avoid empty cars and quiet platforms
recommendedRoutes: Metro E (Expo) Line toward Santa Monica

walking.avoidHours: Late night in residential or beach areas
tips: Rideshare to your hotel whenever in doubt

neighborhoodCards.name: Santa Monica
avoidSpots: Beach paths away from the pier late at night

neighborhoodCards.name: West Hollywood

avoidSpots: Quiet residential side streets off the strip

emergencyBlock.call911: For emergencies requiring immediate police, fire, or medical response.
emergencyBlock.call311: For non-emergency city services and complaints in Los Angeles.
emergencyBlock.nypdPrecinctLocatorText: Find the LAPD station responsible for your area — helpful if you lose something or need quick assistance. Non-emergency line: (877) 275-5273.
emergencyBlock.nearestHospital: LA has multiple 24/7 emergency rooms within 10–15 minutes of the main tourist areas in Santa Monica, Hollywood, and DTLA.
emergencyBlock.subwayHelpPoints: Both Uber and Lyft have in-app emergency buttons that share your location with 911 — use them if needed.

faq.question: Is LA safe to walk around at night?
faq.answer: In tourist areas yes — Santa Monica Promenade, Sunset Strip, Beverly Hills, and Hollywood Blvd are all fine with normal awareness. Avoid walking alone east of Highland on Hollywood Blvd or anywhere near Skid Row.

faq.question: Is Venice Beach safe at night?
faq.answer: The boardwalk is not recommended for solo walking after 10pm. Lighting is inconsistent and the homeless population increases after dark. Santa Monica Pier area is fine until closing time.

faq.question: Should I use Uber at night in LA?
faq.answer: Yes — rideshare is the standard choice for nighttime travel in LA. Most locals drive or rideshare at night. Pedestrians in residential areas after dark can attract unwanted attention.

faq.question: Is West Hollywood safe at night?
faq.answer: Yes — WeHo is one of LA's safest nightlife areas. The Sunset Strip has consistent foot traffic, good lighting, and visible venue security throughout the night.

faq.question: What are the most dangerous areas in LA at night?
faq.answer: Skid Row, parts of East Hollywood, and residential South LA (Compton, Watts, Inglewood) are the highest-risk areas. Tourists have no reason to visit any of these areas at night.

faq.question: Is parking safe at night in LA?
faq.answer: Use attended lots or hotel parking where possible. In multi-story structures, park near the elevator on well-lit floors. Never leave anything in your car — not a bag, jacket, or charger.
